description: Evolution of public opinion on the Paris Climate Agreement from COP21 adoption (Dec 2015) through US withdrawal announcement (June 2017). Global support was strong (~70%+ across nations) but US opinion polarized sharply after Trump withdrawal. Tests geopolitical event impact on domestic opinion polarization.

key_events.description: COP21 in Paris concludes with historic agreement. 195 nations commit to limit warming to 1.5-2°C. Global media celebrates diplomatic achievement.
Earth Day 2016: 175 countries sign Paris Agreement at UN ceremony, largest single-day signing of an international agreement. Public awareness peaks.
Trump wins 2016 US presidential election on platform including Paris withdrawal. Climate becomes partisan identity marker in US.
Trump announces US withdrawal from Paris Agreement (June 1, 2017). Republican support for agreement drops from ~40% to ~25% within weeks.
Yale/George Mason survey shows 69% of US adults support Paris participation, but partisan gap reaches 50+ points (Dem 86% vs Rep 31%). Global support remains above 70%.

calibration_notes: Tests opinion polarization driven by elite cue-taking and partisan identity. US opinion on Paris went from modest bipartisan support to extreme partisan polarization after Trump's withdrawal. Key dynamics: elite signaling driving mass opinion shifts (Republican voters following Trump cue), rally-around-the-flag effect for withdrawal supporters, counter-mobilization by cities/states (#WeAreStillIn), global vs domestic opinion divergence, climate becoming tribal identity issue rather than policy question, and the asymmetry where opposition crystallized faster than support grew. The US-focused trajectory shown here tracks the country where opinion shifted most dramatically.
